bibliographic_data.title: Using Deep Learning to Increase Eye-Tracking Robustness, Accuracy, and Precision in Virtual Reality
bibliographic_data.abstract: Algorithms for the estimation of gaze direction from mobile and video-based eye trackers typically involve tracking a feature of the eye that moves through the eye camera image in a way that covaries with the shifting gaze direction, such as the center or boundaries of the pupil. Tracking these features using traditional computer vision techniques can be difficult due to partial occlusion and environmental reflections. Although recent efforts to use machine learning (ML) for pupil tracking have demonstrated superior results when evaluated using standard measures of segmentation performance, little is known of how these networks may affect the quality of the final gaze estimate. This work provides an objective assessment of the impact of several contemporary ML-based methods for eye feature tracking when the subsequent gaze estimate is produced using either feature -based or model-based methods. Metrics include the accuracy and precision of the gaze estimate, as well as drop-out rate.

core_content.novelty_claim: This work provides an objective assessment of the impact of several contemporary ML-based methods for eye feature tracking on the final gaze estimate in virtual reality applications.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use a custom pipeline to test and report on the impact of several contemporary eye segmentation networks on the spatial accuracy, precision, and robustness to dropouts of the final gaze estimate while keeping other properties of the eye tracking pipeline unchanged. They evaluate two algorithms for gaze mapping: Pupil Labs feature-based gaze mapper and 3D model-based gaze mapper.
outcomes: The study aims to measure how improvements in feature detection models affect the quality of the final gaze estimate.
Metrics include accuracy, precision, and drop-out rate of the gaze estimate.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper explores the use of deep learning for improving eye-tracking robustness, accuracy, and precision in virtual reality. Traditional computer vision techniques struggle with partial occlusions and environmental reflections when tracking features like the pupil center or boundaries. Recent machine learning approaches have shown better results but their impact on final gaze estimates is not well understood. The study evaluates several contemporary ML-based methods for eye feature tracking using a custom pipeline, focusing on accuracy, precision, and dropout rates of the gaze estimate.
